common_name: Fourth of July Tomato (Ultra-Early Hybrid Slicing Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Fourth of July produces ripe tomatoes 49–55 days after transplanting — approximately 2–3 weeks earlier than mid-season varieties. In zone 9, transplant in late February or early March for a first harvest in late April to early May. The very early maturation is particularly valuable in zone 9 where the summer window closes before many standard varieties finish producing.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Fourth of July is an indeterminate hybrid tomato that holds the distinction of being one of the earliest-producing standard-sized tomatoes available to home gardeners — producing its first ripe fruits approximately 49–55 days after transplanting, roughly the same time that slower varieties are just beginning to set fruit. The name references the plant's ability to provide ripe tomatoes by or before the Fourth of July holiday in northern gardens when planted on schedule. The medium-small (4–6 oz), round, bright red fruits have good sweet-tart tomato flavor for an early variety — notably better than most extremely early types, which often sacrifice flavor for speed. The indeterminate vines continue producing throughout the summer and fall after early harvests, unlike determinates that produce one concentrated crop. Fourth of July is outstanding for gardeners in any climate who want fresh home-grown tomatoes as early as possible, and particularly valuable in Gulf South gardens where early production before summer heat peak extends the harvest season significantly. A practical, reliable early variety that should be in every serious tomato garden alongside a mid-season variety.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Fourth of July tomato variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Early tomato variety comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Variety Evaluation

common_name: Provider Bean (Cold-Tolerant Dependable Bush Snap Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow starting 2 weeks before last frost; Provider germinates in cooler soils (as low as 50°F) than most beans, allowing earlier planting. In zone 9, sow February–April for spring production and August–September for fall production. The cold-soil tolerance allows earlier planting and fall planting into cooling soils, extending the productive season at both ends.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep; Provider is one of the most widely grown and recommended bush snap bean varieties in the United States — a 1965 release that has remained a cornerstone home garden and commercial variety for 60 years due to its combination of reliable production, consistent quality, and unusually cold-tolerant germination. Provider's ability to germinate reliably in cooler soils (down to about 50°F, compared to the 60°F minimum for most beans) allows it to be planted 1–2 weeks earlier than standard beans in spring and later into fall, giving it a significant practical advantage for extending the bean season in both directions. The round, medium-length (4–5 inch), dark green pods are harvested when young and tender and have clean snap-bean flavor — honest, straightforward, and pleasant rather than refined. A vigorous, upright bush habit requires no staking and produces heavily over a 2–3 week period per planting; successive sowings every 2–3 weeks provide continuous harvest. Provider has been the standard reliable bush bean against which others are compared — not glamorous, but dependable, productive, and universally adaptable from the earliest spring planting to late fall production in virtually any vegetable garden climate.
sources: University of Missouri Extension — Bean Variety Evaluation
Fedco Seeds — Provider bean variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Snap Bean Variety Trials

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Calypso is a gynoecious (all-female) pickling cucumber that produces an extremely concentrated set of fruits — a male pollenizer (typically a small percentage of open-pollinated seed included in the packet) is required for fruit set. Harvest frequently, every 1–2 days, to maximize production; fruits left on the vine signal the plant to stop producing.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; train vines to a trellis for straighter fruits and improved air circulation. Calypso is a 2009 All-America Selections-winning pickling cucumber that earned recognition for its exceptional disease resistance package and concentrated production — bred to perform reliably in a wide range of climates and conditions where fungal and viral diseases limit cucumber yields. Calypso carries resistance to angular leaf spot, cucumber scab, cucumber mosaic virus, papaya ringspot virus, watermelon mosaic virus, and zucchini yellow mosaic virus, plus tolerance to powdery mildew — one of the most comprehensive disease resistance packages available in a pickling cucumber. The dark green, warted, blocky fruits (3–4 inches for fresh pickle use, 1.5–2 inches for whole baby pickles) have thick, crisp walls and excellent pickle texture that holds up to both quick-process and long-fermented pickle methods. The gynoecious (all-female) breeding concentrates fruit set into a shorter, more predictable harvest window — convenient for processing large pickle batches. An outstanding choice for home pickle-makers, zone 9 summer gardens where disease pressure is high, and any gardener wanting maximum yields with minimum disease management input.
sources: All-America Selections — Calypso cucumber AAS winner 2009
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Calypso variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucumber Production Guide

common_name: Kentucky Blue Pole Bean (AAS-Winning Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; provide a sturdy trellis, pole tepee, or wire fence at least 5–6 feet tall. Kentucky Blue vines climb to 6–7 feet and require strong support. Harvest pods frequently at 5–6 inches for maximum tenderness and continued production.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 60°F; Kentucky Blue is a 1991 All-America Selections-winning pole bean that crossed two of the most beloved pole bean varieties — Kentucky Wonder and Blue Lake — to combine the best traits of both parents. Kentucky Wonder contributes vigor, heavy production, excellent traditional pole bean flavor, and the characteristic meaty pod texture; Blue Lake contributes its renowned stringless quality, mild sweetness, and the refined, crisp texture that made it the gold standard of snap bean flavor. The resulting Kentucky Blue produces long (6–8 inch), round, deep green, completely stringless pods with excellent flavor that tasters consistently rate among the best of all snap beans — more substantial than filet beans but more refined than older Kentucky Wonder strains. Very productive vines climbing 6–7 feet continue bearing throughout the summer and fall when harvested regularly. The combination of classic pole bean character, genuine stringlessness, superior flavor, and heavy production has made Kentucky Blue one of the most popular and recommended pole beans in American gardens since its introduction.
sources: All-America Selections — Kentucky Blue pole bean AAS winner 1991
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Pole bean variety comparison
University of Massachusetts Cooperative Extension — Bean Variety Trials

common_name: Jade Bean (Slender Dark Green French Filet-Style Bush Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; harvest Jade pods when they are 4–5 inches long and still slender — the smaller pods are more tender and flavorful. Unlike standard snap beans, Jade should not be allowed to develop visible seeds inside before harvest, or the pods become fibrous. Regular picking every 2–3 days maximizes quality and production.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Jade is a refined bush snap bean with slender, deep jade-green pods that occupies a quality niche between standard American snap beans and the more delicate French haricot vert types. The pods are round (not flat), uniformly dark green, stringless, and noticeably more tender and flavorful than standard bean varieties when harvested young at 4–5 inches — with a mild, sweet, delicate green bean taste and a fine texture that makes them excellent for light steaming, quick sautéing with butter and herbs, and all preparations where bean flavor is the centerpiece. Unlike the very slender haricot vert types (such as Maxibel or Espada), Jade has a slightly more substantial pod that is easier to harvest and handle in quantity, while still maintaining considerably more refinement than standard snap beans like Blue Lake or Provider. A popular choice for market gardeners, farm-stand vendors, and home gardeners who want a more elegant snap bean without committing to the exacting harvest schedule of true filet-type beans. Very productive and relatively heat-tolerant for a fine-podded bean.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Jade bean variety notes
University of Massachusetts Cooperative Extension — Bean Variety Trials
